data.title: Saudi Arabia's iOud Posts Lower FY24 Net Profit; Revenue Up
data.description:
Intelligent Oud Co. for Trading (SASE:9579), d/b/a iOud, said Thursday that its net profit for full-year 2024 fell, while revenue rose year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 4.7 million Saudi riyals, compared with 5.3 million riyals earlier. EPS moved to 2.84 riyals from 3.09 riyals.

The Saudi Arabia-listed cosmetic products supplier's revenue was 26.7 million riyals, compared with 25.2 million riyals previously.

data.title: China’s 10-Year Bond Yield Drops on High Debt Issuance
data.description:
China’s 10-year government bond yield dropped to around 1.87% on Friday, as the government increased debt issuance aimed at stimulating growth and stabilizing the bond market.

In the first quarter of 2025, the Ministry of Finance raised a record CNY 1.45 trillion through sovereign bonds, three times more than the same period last year and marking a record for any first quarter.

This unprecedented surge in debt issuance highlights Beijing’s growing urgency to ramp up fiscal spending in response to multiple economic pressures, while also seeking to alleviate concerns over last year’s bond rally, which amplified risks to financial stability.

Meanwhile, external pressures have intensified, as US President Donald Trump’s new tariffs on Chinese goods have added to the economic uncertainty.

In response, Chinese policymakers have unveiled plans to issue CNY 11.86 trillion in new government bonds this year, along with raising the budget deficit target to an unprecedented 4% of GDP.

data.title: Som Datt Finance to Raise Up to INR500 Million via Rights Issue
data.description:
Som Datt Finance said its board approved a rights issue of equity shares to raise up to 500 million Indian rupees, according to an Indian bourse filing on Thursday.

The issue is subject to regulatory approvals, the filing said.

data.title: South Carolina dismisses its staking lawsuit against Coinbase, joining Vermont
data.description:
South Carolina has become the latest US state to dismiss its lawsuit against crypto exchange Coinbase over its staking services, which had accused the crypto exchange of offering unregistered securities.

The lawsuit was officially dismissed in a joint stipulation between the crypto exchange and the South Carolina Attorney General’s securities division on March 27.

“South Carolina just joined Vermont to dismiss its unfounded staking lawsuit against Coinbase,” the firm’s chief legal officer, Paul Grewal, said in a March 27 X post.

“This is not just a victory for us, but for American consumers and we hope it's a sign of things to come in the few states left that restrict staking.”

South Carolina and Vermont were two of 10 US states that took legal action against Coinbase's staking services on June 6, 2023 — the same day that the federal securities regulator filed its lawsuit against the crypto exchange.

The Securities and Exchange Commission officially dismissed that lawsuit on Feb. 27, 2025.

The other eight US states that filed enforcement action similar to South Carolina were Alabama, California, Illinois, Kentucky, Maryland, New Jersey, Washington and Wisconsin. 

Grewal said he hoped to see other states follow suit, and that South Carolina residents lost an estimated $2 million in staking rewards as a result of the lawsuit.

“The 52 million Americans who own crypto deserve commonsense consumer protections and clear rules,” he said. “We applaud South Carolina for standing up for justice and hope the remaining states with bans on staking will take notice.”

South Carolina introduces Bitcoin reserve bill

Meanwhile, a state lawmaker has just introduced the “Strategic Digital Assets Reserve Act of South Carolina” on March 27, which could see the state treasurer allocate up to 10% of certain state funds to cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin (BTC).

Unlike most US state crypto reserve bills, North Carolina’s House Bill 4256, introduced by Rep. Jordan Pace, mentioned Bitcoin on several occasions for the Strategic Digital Assets Reserve that the bill seeks to establish.

The bill allows South Carolina’s treasurer, currently Curtis Loftis, to establish a Bitcoin reserve that exceeds no more than 1 million Bitcoin — a high ceiling that the US federal government is also looking to reach or exceed with its recently established Strategic Bitcoin Reserve.

The treasurer would be able to add Bitcoin to South Carolina’s General Fund, the Budget Stabilization Reserve Fund any other investment fund that they manage.

While no mention of stablecoins, non-fungible tokens, Ether (ETH) or any other crypto tokens was made, the House bill said the Strategic Digital Assets Reserve wouldn’t be limited to Bitcoin.

According to Bitcoin Law, 42 Bitcoin reserve bills have been introduced at the state level in 19 states, and 36 of those 42 bills remain live.

Earlier this month, US President Donald Trump signed an executive order to create a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve and a Digital Asset Stockpile, both of which will initially use cryptocurrency forfeited in government criminal cases.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Friday at 1 AM ET
data.description:
Major U.S. Stock Indexes Close Lower After Tariffs Shake Automakers

Stock indexes fell for a second day after the U.S. unveiled new tariffs on automakers.

BOJ Policy Board More Cautious of U.S. Policy Risks

The Bank of Japan's policy board has become more cautious about uncertainties regarding U.S. economic policies, according to the summary of opinions of its March meeting.

Inflation Might Be Stuck for Now. The Fed's Preferred Metric Comes Friday.

Americans finally got some good news in the form of cooling inflation earlier this month. But the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge is set to reverse that trend.

Fed's Collins supports holding interest rates steady for 'longer time'

The U.S. economic outlook is cloudy and the Federal Reserve should be patient about adjusting interest rates, Boston Fed President Susan Collins said Thursday.

Canada Aims to Respond to Latest U.S. Tariffs When Trump's Next Salvo Is Fired

Prime Minister Mark Carney pledged to respond to Trump's latest tariffs targeting the automotive industry with strong counter measures, but held off introducing further tariffs until it becomes clear what the president will introduce next week.

Bank of Mexico Makes Second Straight Half-Point Rate Cut

The Bank of Mexico lowered its benchmark interest rate by half of a percentage point for the second meeting in a row as inflation eased and the economy continued to slow, and said further cuts of that size are possible.

U.S. Fourth-Quarter GDP Revised Higher

The pace of economic growth picked up slightly in the final months of 2024, government data showed, but most economists expect a slowdown in the first quarter.

White House Makes Fresh Economic Demands of Ukraine as Russia Talks Stall

President Trump is putting pressure on Ukraine for an economic deal with greater concessions from Kyiv, just as Washington's efforts to get Russia to agree to a cease-fire have stalled.

Trump's Car Tariffs Are Worse Than the Worst-Case Scenario. GM Stock Tumbles.

President Trump announces 25% tariffs on cars imported to the U.S. from anywhere in the world. The policy is much worse than Wall Street expected.

The U.S. trade deficit remains near a record high - and will be a drag on economic growth

The deficit in February remained much larger than the $135.5 billion trade gap forecast by Wall Street economists polled by Econoday.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Friday at 1 AM ET
data.description:
CoreWeave Prices IPO at $40, Well Below Earlier Estimates

The AI start-up priced its stock at $40, well below recent expectations for $47 to $55 a share. The listing has been seen as a test for both AI and the market for new stock.

Lululemon Outlook Misses as Consumer Confidence Falls

The activewear company said it expects sales of $11.15 billion to $11.3 billion for its fiscal 2025, the high-end of which is in line with analyst views.

Trump Warned U.S. Automakers Not to Raise Prices in Response to Tariffs

The threat came in a call earlier this month, leaving CEOs worried they would face punishment if prices increased.

Signal Ascends From Hacker Passion Project to Washington's Top Messaging App

Downloads of the app surged in the past week after the Atlantic published White House officials' texts about a military strike.

Trump's Car Tariffs Are Worse Than the Worst-Case Scenario. GM Stock Tumbles.

President Trump announces 25% tariffs on cars imported to the U.S. from anywhere in the world. The policy is much worse than Wall Street expected.

Ubisoft Gets $1.25 Billion Investment From Tencent for New Gaming Unit

Ubisoft Entertainment said it is forming a new unit that would house its Assassin's Creed, Far Cry, and Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six games, with Tencent Holdings investing 1.16 billion euros ($1.25 billion) for a minority stake in the subsidiary.

Ferrari to Raise Prices on Some Models in Response to U.S. Tariffs

Ferrari said it will shield customers from the brunt of U.S. tariffs by absorbing the cost of higher import duties on certain models, while passing on only some of the impact through higher pricing on others.

Freight Startup Flexport Misses Profitability Target

Founder and CEO of digital forwarder, Ryan Petersen, says company will hit goal in 2025, paving way for potential public offering.

Repsol to Combine U.K. North Sea Upstream Business With NEO Energy

The joint venture could deliver synergies exceeding $1 billion.

H&M Earnings Miss Forecasts on Higher Discounts and Costs

Sales rose 2% but momentum slowed in the final month of the quarter.

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Markets in Wait-And-See Mode Ahead of Reciprocal Tariffs
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

EU business and consumer surveys; U.K. GDP, trade, retail sales, balance of payments, business investment; Germany Gfk consumer climate survey, unemployment; France CPI, consumer spending; no major corporate trading updates expected

Opening Call:

European stock futures were edging lower in tandem with broad declines in Asian equities. The dollar gained slightly; Treasury yields were mostly lower; Oil futures declined, and gold gained.

Equities:

European futures pointed slightly lower as investors and analysts struggle to digest the impact of the Trump administration's latest tariffs.

The U.S. stock market fell for the second day in a row on Thursday after trading sideways for most of the session.

After sharp losses earlier in the month, bleeding related to tariff news appears to have slowed. Markets are in a bit of a holding pattern as they await the Trump administration's plans for reciprocal tariffs starting on April 2. Some Wall Street analysts are taking solace in a run of decent economic data reports, and it isn't clear how determined Trump is to stick with the tariffs long-term.

Fed officials on Thursday indicated that the central bank was unlikely to change interest rates until the tariff fog starts to clear. On tap today is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge- the personal consumption expenditures price index for February. Economists forecast a 2.5% year-over-year increase, matching the January figure.

Forex:

Sterling could rise modestly against the euro this year as the U.K. economy should outperform the eurozone, Bank of America analysts said. President Trump's tariff plans are targeting countries with large surpluses. The U.K. falls out of this category while it also benefits from a largely service-focused economy, they said.

Europe's fiscal spending pledges are supportive of the euro through flows into European equities. However, this is likely via a rotation from U.S. equities rather than U.K. equities. The European Central Bank could cut interest rates more than markets expect while the Bank of England remains cautious, they said.

BofA expects the euro to fall to 0.8000 pounds by the fourth quarter.

Bonds:

The gilt market's volatility risks resurfacing due to the vulnerable state of U.K. public finances and heavy gilt supply ahead, HSBC U.K. rates strategist Daniela Russell said. Concerns about the impact of tariffs on the U.K. economy could also weigh on investor sentiment, she said.

"Attention can now shift back towards the macroeconomic data and global developments, including around possible reciprocal U.S. tariffs, and how they will affect the Bank of England outlook," she said.

Energy:

Oil prices were lower early Friday. The oil market could see growing volatility and pullback risks, said Maria Agustina Patti, a financial markets strategist consultant at Exness. Prices could be supported by tighter global supply concerns, driven by U.S. tariff threats on Venezuelan oil buyers and ongoing sanctions on Iranian oil, Patti said.

Traders may stay cautious about any developments that may exacerbate supply disruptions, Patti added.

Metals:

Gold was slightly higher after hitting an all-time high overnight. "A series of economic and geopolitical factors have fueled this surge," XS.com's Antonio Di Giacomo said. "Among them, the new tariff measures announced by U.S. President Trump stand out, as they have created uncertainty in international markets," the financial markets analyst said.

This protectionist stance has boosted trade tensions and spurred investors to seek refuge in safe-haven assets like gold, the analyst added.

--

Copper prices fell amid profit-taking. Expectations that the Trump administration will announce a copper-specific levy in the coming weeks has fueled a recent rise in prices, ANZ Research analysts said.

However, any pullback in copper prices is likely to be relatively short-lived, with strong fundamentals providing a backstop. China's copper imports are strong while inventories are falling, it noted.

--

Iron ore prices were lower, weighed by subdued demand. Analysts remain cautious that the current demand may not sustain given regulatory policies, Baocheng Futures said. However, iron ore's strong fundamentals could support prices ahead, it added.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Inflation Might Be Stuck for Now. The Fed's Preferred Metric Comes Friday.

Americans finally received some good news in the form of cooling inflation earlier this month. But the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge is set to reverse that trend with the latest data release on Friday.

Earlier in the month, the latest data from the Consumer Price Index and Producer Price Index provided Americans some relief that overall consumer and wholesale pricing pressures eased in February. But under the hood, factors from both indexes that the Bureau of Economic Analysis uses to calculate the Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index point to a firmer February print for the Fed's preferred measure.

Fed's Collins supports holding interest rates steady for 'longer time'

The U.S. economic outlook is cloudy and the Federal Reserve should be patient about adjusting interest rates, Boston Fed President Susan Collins said Thursday.

"I strongly believed at the meeting that we had last week that holding our rate steady was an appropriate policy stance and, looking forward, I would expect holding it steady for a longer time is likely to be appropriate," Collins said in a moderated discussion sponsored by the Insurance Women's Investment Network, 100 Women in Finance and Wellington Management

Ferrari to Raise Prices on Some Models in Response to U.S. Tariffs

Ferrari plans price increases of up to 10% on some of its models after the introduction of U.S. auto tariffs, as the sports-car maker races to mitigate a hit from the duties.

U.S. President Trump said he will impose 25% tariffs on all vehicles imported to the country from April 2, a move that will hit Ferrari more than most European manufacturers as it only produces its cars in Italy.

Ubisoft Gets $1.25 Billion Investment From Tencent for New Gaming Unit

Ubisoft Entertainment said it is forming a new unit that would house its Assassin's Creed, Far Cry, and Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six games, with Tencent Holdings investing 1.16 billion euros ($1.25 billion) for a minority stake in the subsidiary.

The French videogame company said Thursday that the deal with Tencent values the newly formed subsidiary at an enterprise value-including debt-of EUR4 billion.

CoreWeave Prices IPO at $40, Well Below Estimates. It's Another Headwind for AI and Public Markets.

CoreWeave, a rapidly growing AI cloud company, priced its initial public offering at $40 a share on Thursday night, well below an expected range of $47 to $55.

At the $40 IPO price, CoreWeave has a market value of $23 billion. The company also sold 23% fewer shares than it originally intended.

Trump Warned U.S. Automakers Not to Raise Prices in Response to Tariffs

When President Trump convened CEOs of some of the country's top automakers for a call earlier this month, he issued a warning: They better not raise car prices because of tariffs.

Trump told the executives that the White House would look unfavorably on such a move, leaving some of them rattled and worried they would face punishment if they increased prices, people with knowledge of the call said.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: SingTel's Tech Services Arm, Globe Telecom to Form Joint Venture
data.description:
SingTel's technology services arm, NCS, entered into a joint venture with Philippine-based Globe Telecom , according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Thursday.

Under the deal, NCS agreed to acquire a majority stake in Yondu, the IT arm of Globe Telecom.

The joint venture, to be renamed NCS Philippines, is expected to increase its workforce in the Philippines to more than 1,200 professionals and accelerate the expansion of NCS' global delivery network.

data.title: Ve Wong Unit Decides Against Paying Dividend
data.description:
Ve Wong unit Tai Ve will not distribute dividends, following a board resolution, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The food and beverage company did not disclose further details regarding the decision.

data.title: BP to Proceed with Fourth Subsea Project Amid New Gas Discovery in Trinidad and Tobago
data.description:
BP (BP.L) said Thursday its local arm made a final investment decision on its fourth subsea project in Trinidad and Tobago amid a new gas discovery at its Frangipani well.

The Ginger subsea project will include four subsea wells and subsea trees that will be connected to bp Trinidad and Tobago's existing Mahogany B platform. The first gas is expected in 2027.

Meanwhile, the oil and gas company said drilling at its fully owned Frangipani well identified multiple stacked gas reservoirs within the same geological structure. BP is now assessing options for the discovery.

The company said both the new developments support its objective of growing its oil and gas business.

data.title: Eurofins Raises EUR400 Million from Hybrid Bond Offer
data.description:
Eurofins Scientific said Thursday it raised 400 million euros from its oversubscribed offering of a euro-denominated hybrid bond.

The bond, scheduled to be issued on April 4, 2025, is subject to a fixed annual coupon rate of 5.75% until April 4, 2032, with a first call date on Jan. 4, 2032. The bioanalytical testing company said the bond has no specified maturity date.

Eurofins said proceeds from the issuance may be used for its ongoing offer to redeem hybrid bonds due on Nov. 13, for which a tender offer is scheduled to close April 2. Any remaining amount is earmarked for general corporate expenditures.

data.title: Thai, South Korean stocks sink on US auto tariffs
data.description:
Key points:
  • South Korea's stocks fall about 2%
  • Thai stocks hit one-week low, baht weakens 0.4%
  • Singapore's Straits Times index breaches 4,000 level
  • Indonesian markets closed for national holiday

South Korean and Thailand equities tumbled on Friday after new U.S. tariffs on auto imports jolted car and auto parts makers, while participants in Asian emerging markets braced for the reciprocal U.S. tariffs set to take effect next week.

Thailand's benchmark index slid 1% to a one-week low and the baht weakened 0.4%, as Southeast Asia's biggest auto production centre and an export base for top car makers stood exposed to the new U.S. auto tariffs.

"The U.S.'s higher import tariffs on automobiles and auto parts will weigh further on Thailand's ailing economy via an additional drag on the automotive sector," said Charnon Boonnuch, an ASEAN economist at Nomura, noting that the reciprocal and auto sector tariffs announced were a downside risk to Thailand's growth outlook.

South Korea's KOSPI toppled nearly 2% in its second straight day of losses, dragged lower by sharp losses in Hyundai Motor and sister automaker Kia Corp .

The index has lost more than 3% over the past two sessions as the two automakers lost billions of dollars in value.

"Autos account for 9.4% of Asia's exports to the U.S., but the exposure is highest for South Korea and Japan," said Sonal Varma, Chief Economist for India and Asia excluding Japan at Nomura.

Taiwan's benchmark index TWSE:TAIEX also slid nearly 2% to its weakest level since mid-September last year. TSMC , the world's top contract chipmaker, plumbed to its mid-September low point.

The Korean won and Taiwan's dollar slipped around 0.3% each.

An MSCI gauge of Asia EM equities (.MIMS00000PUS) skidded 0.8%, while a broader gauge of world EM equities CBOE:EFS was down 0.7%.

While analysts anticipate limited direct effects from U.S. tariffs on trade-reliant Southeast Asian economies, they remain vulnerable to reciprocal U.S. tariffs and slower growth in the global economy and key trading partners.

Singapore's Straits Times index (STI) breached the 4,000 level for the first time, led higher by a rally in top banks.

This is a "key psychological milestone, which could be interpreted as strong investor confidence, resilient blue-chip performance, and a favourable economic climate," said Daphne Tan, Director of Business Development at CMC Markets Singapore.

"Breaking this level suggests a strengthening sentiment, but sustaining it would depend on global stability and market fundamentals."

Malaysia's ringgit (.MYR=), Singapore's dollar , and the Philippine peso edged lower. India's rupee appreciated 0.15%, while its Nifty 50 index was largely unchanged in early trade.

Indonesia's financial markets , remain closed for a national holiday till April 7.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** Trump Organization eyes multi-billion-dollar projects in Vietnam amid tariff risks

** Philippines' trade deficit narrows to $3.15 bln in Feb

** Indonesia's Widjaja family offers to take Sinarmas Land private in $986 million deal

** Malaysia's 10-year bond yield (MY10YT=RR) hovers at 3-week high of 3.781%

data.title: CaixaBank Board Approves Deputy Chair Appointment
data.description:
CaixaBank said Thursday its board agreed to appoint María Martínez as deputy chairwoman.

The approval follows a favorable report from the Spanish bank's appointments and sustainability committee. Martínez currently serves as an independent director at the bank.

data.title: Accordant Group Took Cost Out of Business to Soften Fiscal 2025 Loss
data.description:
Accordant Group said it took significant cost out of the business to soften an expected relatively smaller loss for the fiscal year ending March 31, compared to the previous year, with resulting revenues down around 23% year-over-year, according to a Friday New Zealand bourse filing.

It said that an increase in business and consumer confidence aligned to easing inflationary pressures was not felt in time to stimulate the market, reduce unemployment, or lift the group's fiscal second-half performance.

The company expects a more positive trading environment in the second half of the calendar year, the filing noted.

data.title: Longfor Group Posts Decline in Net Profit, Revenue Amid Property Slump
data.description:
By Jiahui Huang

Chinese property developer Longfor Group's net profit and revenue declined in 2024 amid a continued sector downturn in the world's second-largest economy.

The Beijing-based company's net profit fell 19% to 10.40 billion yuan, or equivalent of $1.43 billion, compared with the previous year. Its revenue dropped to 127.47 billion from 180.74 billion yuan in 2023.

Revenue from Longfor's core property development business fell 35% to 100.77 billion yuan.

While revenue from its property development and services segment declined last year, revenue from its investment property operation and property service segments rose 7.4% to CNY26.71 billion.

Longfor is one of the few Chinese developers that have been increasing profits from asset management and investment properties instead of homebuilding, as the country's property sector remains mired in a years-long downturn.

Looking ahead, Longfor said it will focus on reducing inventory in its property development business while replenishing land and improving its land bank structure.

The company declared a final dividend of CNY0.10 per share for the full year 2024.

Write to Jiahui Huang at jiahui.huang@wsj.com

data.description:
Apple Inc :

data.title: Wilmar International Achieves Net Zero Targets
data.description:
Wilmar International's near and long term emissions reduction targets were validated by the Science Based Targets initiative, according to a company release with the Singapore Exchange on Thursday.

The targets were validated under the SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard and the SBTi Forest, Land, and Agriculture (FLAG) Guidance, along with scope 3 emissions.

Wilmar has committed to reduce Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions by 50.4% by 2032 and has targeted absolute reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 2050.

data.description:

data.title: Lana Co Posts Higher FY24 Net Profit, Revenue
data.description:
Lana Co (SASE:9587) said Thursday its net profit and revenue for full-year 2024 surged year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 8 million Saudi riyals, compared with 4.8 million riyals earlier. EPS moved to 2.22 riyals from 1.59 riyals.

The Saudi Arabia-listed medical device company's revenue was 84.1 million riyals, compared with 48.6 million riyals a year ago.

data.title: dKargo (DKA) - Testnet - 31 Mar 2025
data.description:
dKargo is launching a testnet, an important step in blockchain development. A successful test could increase confidence in the technology, attracting more investors and possibly driving the price up. However, testnets often reveal technical challenges, and negative results may lead to a price drop. Investors should monitor test outcomes closely. Testnet success sets the stage for a mainnet launch, which could be a stronger price catalyst. Thus, the testnet’s impact may be mixed until further developments are clear. source

dKargo
@dKargo_Official

Mark your calendars. ️

The dKargo Testnet is going live on 31st March 2025.

Mar 27, 2025

data.description:
SoSoValue is launching its API Open Platform, which allows developers to build on the SOSO ecosystem. This could increase demand for SOSO tokens as new applications or services may drive user engagement. If the platform attracts significant interest, we might see a higher trading volume, potentially leading to a price increase. However, without clear indications of developer interest, the impact on price could be limited. Watch market sentiment closely before investing. source

data.title: Crypto Market Cap Evolution Shows Diverging Trends Among Top Digital Asset
data.description:
The crypto market continues to evolve as shifts in market capitalization among major digital assets reflect both investor sentiment and fundamental developments.

A recent analysis from CryptoQuant provides a closer look at how top crypto have performed in terms of market cap and drawdowns over the past several months.

BNB, XRP, and Ethereum Show Diverging Trends

One of the most notable shifts has been Binance Coin (BNB) reclaiming its position as the fifth-largest cryptocurrency by market capitalization. BNB’s market cap rose to approximately $92 billion, surpassing Solana (SOL), which now sits at $74 billion.

This shift follows a strong rally in SOL during late 2024, largely fueled by growth in its meme coin ecosystem. However, the attention of speculative activity appears to have transitioned toward the BNB Chain, where similar crypto ecosystem momentum has helped support its recovery.

Another significant market cap development involves XRP. According to CryptoQuant, XRP’s market capitalization increased substantially following the 2024 US presidential election.

CryptoQuant.com
@cryptoquant_com

Market Cap Evolution of Top Cryptocurrencies

BNB and Bitcoin are currently experiencing the lowest drawdowns among this group, each down approximately 20% from their all-time highs, indicating relatively strong price performance and resilience. pic.twitter.com/dW3tXlvSMG

Mar 27, 2025

From a valuation of $30 billion in early November, XRP’s market cap climbed to $141 billion by March 2025. The timing of this rise appears to align with the outcome of the US election, which some believe could potentially influence regulatory sentiment around crypto assets.

In contrast, Ethereum (ETH) has faced a more challenging trajectory. After peaking in late 2024, ETH’s market capitalization declined by 50% to around $240 billion as of March 2025.

This sharp drop highlights the current volatility in the altcoin market and raises questions about Ethereum’s ability to maintain its prior valuation levels amid shifting macro and sector-specific factors.

Crypto Price Resilience and Drawdown Metrics

CryptoQuant’s report also evaluated drawdowns—the decline from an asset’s all-time high—as a measure of relative performance. Bitcoin (BTC) and BNB emerged as the most resilient assets among the group, each down approximately 20% from their respective all-time highs.

BNB’s stability has been linked to its continued utility within the Binance ecosystem, including usage for transaction fees and platform-related activities.

Meanwhile, ETH and SOL have struggled to recover from deeper drawdowns. Both assets are currently more than 50% below their previous peaks, highlighting higher levels of volatility and reduced investor momentum.

Although XRP has seen a rise in market capitalization, its price still reflects a drawdown of roughly 36%, indicating that much of the new capital inflow has yet to translate into price recovery.

Featured image created with DALL-E, Chart from TradingView

data.title: Alqemam for Computer Systems' FY24 Net Profit, Revenue Fall
data.description:
Alqemam for Computer Systems (SASE:9558) said Thursday its net profit and revenue for full-year 2024 declined year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 9.3 million Saudi riyals, compared with 18.9 million riyals earlier. EPS moved to 3.08 riyals from 6.29 riyals.

The Saudi Arabia-listed IT service management company's revenue was 49.7 million riyals, compared with 51.9 million riyals a year ago.

data.title: Oil holds near one-month high, set for third week of gains amid supply woes
data.description:
Key points:
  • Brent, WTI up 2% so far this week
  • Concerns over Venezuelan, Iranian oil exports
  • But price gains likely capped as tariffs hit demand

Oil prices eased a touch but held near one-month highs on Friday as they headed for a third weekly gain on a tightening global supply outlook after the U.S. imposed tariffs against countries buying oil from Venezuela and placed curbs on Iranian oil trade.

Brent crude futures lost 14 cents, or 0.2%, at $73.89 a barrel. U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures were down 12 cents, or 0.2%, to $69.80 a barrel at 0431 GMT.

The moves were minor compared with the gains of over 2% for both contracts so far this week. They are up more than 7% since hitting multi-month lows in early March.

The main driver of the price rally has been the shifting landscape of global oil sanctions, BMI analysts wrote in a market commentary.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday announced new 25% tariffs on potential buyers of Venezuelan crude, days after U.S. sanctions targeting China's imports from Iran.

The order added fresh uncertainty to buyers and saw trade of Venezuelan oil to top buyer China stall. Elsewhere, sources said India's Reliance Industries , operator of the world's biggest refining complex, will halt Venezuelan oil imports.

"The potential loss of Venezuelan crude exports to the market due to secondary tariffs and the possibility of the same being imposed on Iranian barrels has caused an apparent tightness in crude supply," said June Goh, a senior oil analyst at Sparta Commodities.

Oil was also underpinned by signs of better demand in the U.S., the world's top oil consumer, as the country's crude stocks fell more than anticipated.

Data by the Energy Information Administration showed U.S. crude inventories fell by 3.3 million barrels to 433.6 million barrels in the week ended March 21, compared with analysts' expectations in a Reuters poll for a 956,000-barrel draw.

The broader global dynamics for oil trade, however, pointed to a period of heightened uncertainty, as a blitz of U.S. tariffs against trading partner countries raises fears of a sharp economic downturn in a blow to oil demand.

As a result, analysts don't expect sharp gains in oil prices to be sustained in the current environment.

"While the market is suffering under extreme uncertainties, we are holding to our forecast for Brent crude to average $76 per barrel in 2025, down from $80 per barrel in 2024," the BMI analysts wrote.

data.title: Dongyue's 2024 Profit Rises; Shares Slide 4%
data.description:
Dongyue's attributable profit rose to 810.8 million yuan in 2024 from 707.8 million yuan in 2023, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

Earnings per share at the chemical manufacturer increased to 0.46 yuan from 0.32 yuan a year earlier. Analysts at Visible Alpha estimated EPS at 0.65 yuan.

Revenue, however, declined to 14.2 billion yuan from 14.5 billion yuan a year earlier. Visible Alpha analysts forecast revenue of 15.9 billion yuan.

The company declared a final dividend of HK$0.1 per share, payable July 10 to shareholders on record as of June 16.

Shares slipped 4% during Friday's afternoon trading.

data.title: Tadweeer's FY24 Net Profit, Revenue Climb
data.description:
National Environmental Recycling Co (SASE:9540), d/b/a Tadweeer, said Thursday that its net profit and revenue for full-year 2024 grew year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 36.2 million Saudi riyals, compared with 25.8 million riyals earlier. EPS moved to 0.63 riyal from 0.43 riyal.

The Saudi Arabia-listed electrical products recycling company's revenue was 806 million riyals, compared with 631.9 million riyals previously.
